# Vyre Thesis

## What vyre is

Vyre is a substrate-neutral primitive-composition IR. It owns a graph
structure and a small set of trait contracts. It owns nothing else.

A Vyre program is a directed graph of operations over memory regions.
The graph is substrate-agnostic: it does not know whether its operations
will execute on a GPU via WGSL, on a CPU via the reference interpreter,
on a Metal device, on a CUDA device, on a photonic accelerator, or on a
substrate that does not yet exist. Workgroup sizes, buffer binding models,
dispatch geometry, and shading-language choices are not Vyre's concerns;
they belong to the backend that executes the graph.

A Vyre primitive is a struct implementing `NodeKind`. The workspace ships
twenty canonical primitives — scan, reduce, shuffle, gather, scatter,
pattern_match_literal, pattern_match_dfa, hash_blake3, hash_fnv1a,
compare_eq, compare_lt, arith_add, arith_mul, bitwise_and, bitwise_or,
bitwise_xor, shift_left, shift_right, popcount, clz — each with a
pure-Rust reference implementation and exhaustive verification on
bounded domains. Third parties register additional primitives through
the same trait; the core does not need to know they exist.

## What vyre is not

Vyre is not a GPU compiler. It is not a tensor DSL. It is not a shading
language frontend. It is not a testing framework. It is not a rule
engine. Downstream crates build those things *by composing Vyre
primitives*. `yaragpu` will be a wrapper around Vyre that composes
pattern-match and hash primitives to execute YARA rules on a GPU.
`tensor-ml` will be a wrapper that composes reduce and gather primitives
into a tensor frontend. Vyre does not ship any of that machinery.
Downstream composition is the whole point.

## Vyre does not touch CPU

Vyre is a GPU substrate. Every dispatch target Vyre ships executes on a
GPU — wgpu today, Metal / CUDA / photonic tomorrow. Vyre does not
implement CPU fallback, automatic CPU↔GPU dispatch by input size, nor
any runtime decision that a given workload is "too small for GPU" and
should run on the host. Consumers that want CPU fallback build it
themselves, outside Vyre, and switch backends at call time.

`vyre-reference` exists as the verification oracle: a pure-Rust CPU
reference implementation used by `vyre-conform` to prove that every
GPU backend produces bit-identical output to a deterministic ground
truth. It is not a dispatch target. No consumer is expected to
`backend::execute` into `vyre-reference` for production work. The
reference interpreter exists so backends can be proven correct; that
is its only contract.

A consumer that wants "run on GPU above N elements, else CPU" composes
two execution crates. Vyre supplies the GPU side. The CPU side is the
consumer's concern. This is deliberate: CPU execution has decades of
mature tooling (Rayon, Cranelift, LLVM backends) that Vyre does not
attempt to match, and attempting to would split Vyre's focus.

## Why primitives are the contract

Because primitives are small and orthogonal, the verification budget
fits the problem. A reduce over 8-bit inputs has 256^n reachable states
for arity n; a two-input reduce has 65 536 states, which is exhaustively
enumerable in milliseconds. Associativity on 8-bit triples is
16 777 216 checks, which runs in seconds. The word "certification"
therefore carries weight: on bounded domains, Vyre's claim is not
property-based testing dressed up as a guarantee, it is enumeration.
Primitives whose input domains exceed the exhaustive budget report this
honestly and degrade to committed random witnesses with explicit
coverage gaps documented per primitive.

This is only possible because a primitive does one thing. The moment a
primitive tries to be two things, its state space explodes and the
exhaustive-verification contract breaks. Composing two small, verified
primitives gives you a large, verified program for free. Building a
large, "verified" primitive gives you property-based testing with a
marketing problem.

## Crate topology

```
vyre-ir          — Program graph, MemoryRegion, NodeKind trait,
                   Backend trait, serialization. Zero runtime deps.
                   Zero knowledge of any backend. Pure IR library.

vyre-primitives  — The 20 canonical NodeKind structs, each with
                   inventory::submit registration. Depends on vyre-ir.

vyre-reference   — Pure-Rust CPU interpreter. The oracle. One reference
                   implementation per primitive. Depends on vyre-ir +
                   vyre-primitives. No backend deps.

vyre-wgpu        — One backend. Lives in the same repo for now but
                   declares dependencies by version, not path, so it is
                   ready to move to a separate repository without a
                   refactor. Implements the Backend trait via a naga
                   AST emitter — no string concatenation.

vyre-conform     — External test harness that imports core + primitives
                   + reference + every available backend. Tests parity
                   between backends and the reference. Never a core
                   dependency. Tests do not live in vyre-core.

vyre             — Umbrella crate. Re-exports the commonly used items
                   from vyre-ir + vyre-primitives + vyre-reference.
                   Backend-free by default. Callers who want a backend
                   add it explicitly: `cargo add vyre vyre-wgpu`.
```

The architectural invariant is enforced by CI: a pull request that
introduces a dependency from `vyre-ir`, `vyre-primitives`, or
`vyre-reference` to `vyre-conform` or any backend crate (outside
`[dev-dependencies]` of test-only harnesses) fails the build.

## The Backend trait

```rust
pub trait Backend: Send + Sync {
    fn id(&self) -> &'static str;
    fn version(&self) -> &'static str;
    fn execute(
        &self,
        program: &Program,
        inputs: &[MemoryRef],
        config: &DispatchConfig,
    ) -> Result<Vec<Memory>, BackendError>;
}
```

Nothing else. A backend does not speak WGSL to the trait. A backend does
not expose compiled-pipeline types to the trait. A backend may cache
compiled artifacts internally, but that is an implementation detail of
the backend crate and never appears in the Vyre public surface.

A new backend is a new crate that implements this trait and submits a
`BackendRegistration` via `inventory::submit!`. A Metal backend, a CUDA
backend, a photonic backend, and a backend for a processor that does not
yet exist all fit the same three-method contract. Vyre core never
changes to support them.

## The Program type

```rust
pub struct Program {
    pub graph: DirectedGraph<Box<dyn NodeKind>>,
    pub memory_regions: Vec<MemoryRegion>,
    pub version: u32,
}

pub struct MemoryRegion {
    pub id: RegionId,
    pub access: Access,       // Read | Write | ReadWrite | Atomic | Shared
    pub element: DataType,
    pub shape: RegionShape,   // Dense(u64) | Sparse | VarLen | CSR { nodes }
}
```

There is no `workgroup_size` on Program. Workgroup sizing is a backend
dispatch-geometry decision and lives in `DispatchConfig`. There is no
`entry_op_id` on Program; the DAG has an explicit output set. There is
no `buffers: Arc<[BufferDecl]>` on Program; buffer-binding models are
backend concerns and the abstract memory regions live independently of
any binding scheme.

A program serialized as wire format carries a version number. Schema
evolutions provide a migration path from `v_n` to `v_{n+1}`. Downstream
consumers who saved replay logs six months ago can still load them.

## Memory ordering

Every atomic operation in Vyre declares its `MemoryOrdering` explicitly:
Relaxed, Acquire, Release, AcqRel, or SeqCst. Every barrier carries the
ordering it establishes. The reference interpreter enforces the
contract: a program that relies on an ordering stronger than what it
declared will, under a deterministic schedule permutation, produce
observable race outputs and fail its test. Vyre does not pretend memory
ordering is a CPU-specific concern that GPU programs can ignore — on
the contrary, the abstraction means a program that is correct on one
substrate must be correct on every substrate that satisfies the same
ordering contract.

## What downstream composition looks like

A consumer builds a domain tool by composing primitives. The
pattern-matching GPU crate `yaragpu` is roughly:

```rust
use vyre::build::{gather, pattern_match_dfa, compare_eq, bitwise_and};
use vyre_wgpu::WgpuBackend;

let program = vyre::Program::new()
    .gather(scan_buffer)
    .pattern_match_dfa(compiled_yara_dfa)
    .compare_eq(rule_id_match)
    .emit();

let backend = WgpuBackend::acquire()?;
let hits = backend.execute(&program, &inputs, &config)?;
```

`yaragpu` does not fork Vyre. It does not reach into Vyre's internals.
It imports primitives, composes them, and calls one trait method. A
Metal port of `yaragpu` requires zero changes to `yaragpu` itself —
the user swaps `WgpuBackend::acquire()` for `MetalBackend::acquire()`.

A Vyre backend that implements a new optimization (shader caching,
incremental compilation, fused-kernel recognition) exposes it as an
opt-in capability that its factory advertises. A consumer asks for a
backend whose capability set includes what it needs. Vyre core never
participates in this negotiation.

## What a new substrate costs

Adding a new substrate to Vyre is one crate. The crate:

1. Implements the `Backend` trait with one `execute` method.
2. Implements lowering from the abstract Program graph to whatever the
   substrate speaks (Metal shaders, CUDA kernels, photonic circuits,
   an FPGA bitstream).
3. Submits a `BackendRegistration` via `inventory::submit!`.
4. Publishes itself on crates.io.

Zero changes to `vyre-ir`. Zero changes to `vyre-primitives`. Zero
changes to `vyre-reference`. Zero changes to any existing backend crate.
Zero changes to `vyre-conform`, which automatically runs its parity
tests against the new backend because it iterates every registered
backend.

This is the meaning of substrate neutrality: a substrate that did not
exist when Vyre was written fits Vyre without any retroactive edit to
Vyre. The existing codebase is therefore incomplete if any of the
following is true: a closed enum constrains what a primitive can be; a
trait method constrains what a substrate must speak; a build script
constrains how a primitive must be declared; a crate dependency
constrains where a test may live. Every such constraint is a bug to rip
out, not to document.

## What this costs in the short term

The rebuild breaks every caller that relied on `Expr`-as-closed-enum
pattern matching, every caller that read `program.workgroup_size`, and
every call to `VyreBackend::dispatch_wgsl`. These breakages are
intentional. They are the cost of abstraction that was promised and
owed. The migration is mechanical in every case because the new API is
narrower, not different — a caller who matched on `Expr::BinOp` now
downcasts to the `BinOp` struct; a caller who read `workgroup_size`
now takes it as a `DispatchConfig` field; a caller who called
`dispatch_wgsl` now constructs a Program and calls `execute`. Every
migration is a single commit that removes the old usage and adds the
new.

No deprecation shim. No dual-path. No "migrate gradually over three
releases." The architecture is either open or it is not.
